context.title: Automating Around a Design Skills Gap
context.description: The older feature images on this site are rough. I'm not a designer and I'm not trying to become one. Closing the skills gap with automation, dropping the friction of shipping posts, and growing a design system by artificial selection.
context.suggestedPrompt: Abstract grid composition of nine square panels, each showing a slightly different geometric variation — a population of candidates under selection pressure. Flat vector style with subtle gradients. Muted palette of deep indigo, teal, and warm off-white. Minimal lines, no text, no literal machinery or AI imagery. Slight grain texture. Evokes iteration, refinement, and design-system evolution without being on-the-nose.
context.notes: Post is about the feature-image pipeline itself — skills-gap + friction framing; evolving design system via artificial selection. Avoid cliché AI/ML imagery (no brains, circuits, robots). The grid-of-variants concept visualizes selection pressure literally; alternative directions: a branching/lineage tree, or a flowing pipeline of discrete stages. Feel free to iterate prompts.

context.userFeedback: This is better than the previous iteration, but I wish the geometric pattern wasn't so regular.

snapshot.subtitle: Phase 12 proof
snapshot.prompt: Abstract grid composition of nine square panels, each showing a slightly different geometric variation — a population of candidates under selection pressure. Flat vector style with subtle gradients. Muted palette of deep indigo, teal, and warm off-white. Minimal lines, no text, no literal machinery or AI imagery. Slight grain texture. Evokes iteration, refinement, and design-system evolution without being on-the-nose.

context.userFeedback: I really like this version. How can we select this as a preferred variation as part of our evolution by artificial selection paradigm?

snapshot.title: Title TK
snapshot.subtitle: Phase 12 proof
snapshot.prompt: Irregular cluster of geometric panels at varied scales and orientations — a loose population of candidates under selection pressure, not arranged on a grid. Flat vector style with subtle gradients. Muted palette of deep indigo, teal, and warm off-white. Organic spacing, overlapping shapes at slight angles, some panels fading at their edges. Minimal lines, no text, no literal machinery or AI imagery. Slight grain texture. Evokes iteration, refinement, and organic variation without being on-the-nose.

context.title: AI Can Generate Your Feature Images. It Can't Design Your Site.
context.description: Most AI-generated feature image workflows stop at the generator. That's the easy half. The missing infrastructure is a human gate cheap enough to actually use, and a design system that gets sharper every time you use it.
context.suggestedPrompt: Abstract editorial proof-mark constellation, scattered minimalist line-drawing glyphs that read as copy-editor symbols without being literal, rendered in parchment-cream with occasional signal-green chartreuse highlights on a deep cool ink-near-black background, dry-ink quality to the line work, faint underlying grid ruling that softens toward the edges, arrangement clustered in the upper two-thirds with generous negative space below, publication-dark atmosphere, a sense of selection pressure — some marks brighter than others, as if a choice is being made, no text, no words, no letters, no typography, no labels
context.notes: Site: editorialcontrol. Thesis of the post is the human gate + prompt-library selection pressure; proof-constellation reads directly as review/selection glyphs and was chosen as baseline. tracked-changes and margin-rules are alternates for AI-iteration or publication-infrastructure framings. All three filter matches are editorialcontrol-tagged with zero usage — they'll all float to top of the picker. Post tags: AI, Automation, Design, Workflow (matched only workflow against proof-constellation — sample set is new).
